TextENGNew: ...Should I have stayed a little longer, back then?
changeReason: OLD uses plural 'we'; NEW uses singular 'I', matching the JPN narrator's personal reflection about their own choice.

TextENGNew: Out of consideration for Rena's state of mind, Rika-chan and I had left a note and withdrawn—but from that point the situation advanced rapidly, and in the worst possible direction.

Standing before a roomful of brand-new furniture that had been hauled inside, Rena remained there in a daze for a time—but eventually she came back to herself, went to check the safe where the bankbooks were kept... and there she discovered the truth.
...There she learned that her father had been pouring enormous sums of money into Rina Mamiya.

And then, finding an apartment brochure mixed in among the papers, she visited the real estate agent in Okinomiya—and at last understood that Rina Mamiya intended to swindle her father out of his money.
—If only she didn't exist... if only she didn't—!!
Rena steeled her resolve, and alone, she planned to kill Rina Mamiya.

And while she continued to act as though nothing were wrong in front of us, she successfully lured Rina Mamiya to her hiding place in the garbage heap—where she split open Rina's skull with a hatchet and killed her.
...As a result, Teppei Houjou could no longer remain in Okinomiya and returned to Hinamizawa, and Satoko was taken back to the Houjou household by her uncle's hand.
When Rena learned of that, she said she would not forgive anyone who tried to destroy her daily life—and resolved to kill Teppei.
...Events moved so swiftly and abnormally that Rika-chan and I had no chance to stop Rena in time.

And then... when we finally learned the truth of what Rena had done, though it was already too late—wanting to protect her if nothing else, we borrowed Mion's help and assisted in concealing what had happened.
...We never for a moment believed that was the right thing to do—but with no way to accept handing Rena over to the police, no other option remained.
...Even so, Rena's violence did not stop.
Everything she had believed in—even God—had betrayed her. Perhaps her mind had already begun to break.

Rena could no longer trust anyone. Tormented by the fear that at any moment one of us might report her to the police, she finally turned her hands against Mion and killed her. Then Shion. Then Satoko. And then...
changeReason: OLD omits the subsequent victims listed in JPN: after Mion, Shion and Satoko were also killed (次は詩音。そして沙都子。そして…); NEW includes these.

TextENGNew: —After Keiichi-kun, Rika-chan is next. So don't worry. Ahahahahaha...!
Those were the last words I heard from Rena.
``Still... Oyashiro-sama, huh. If there really is such a being, they certainly made things complicated for us.``
changeReason: OLD restructures the sentence losing JPN's direct statement about Oyashiro-sama creating complications (余計なことをしてくれた); also changes the conditional framing.

TextENGNew: "Mii... Oyashiro-sama is not that kind of god. A god who wishes suffering upon people—that can't be right."
"Fair enough. ...But the way Rena was frightened—that wasn't normal. What on earth could have..."
Murmuring that, I happened to glance upward at the ceiling—and in that moment, a certain scene floated up in my mind.
That time—when I was consumed by fear of Oyashiro-sama, no longer certain what was true, no longer able to trust anything.
And then—when I was crushed and overwhelmed by the weight of my own sinful mistake—the one who gently extended a precious hand to me was... without any doubt...!
"—Keiichi?"
"...Hm, ...oh, sorry. In any case—even if we say something to Rena now it'll be difficult. So let's think of a different approach that doesn't make her anxious."
"...A different approach? What would that be?"
"Well... I'll have to think about it."
"Ow?! R-Rika-chan, what are you doing?"
"...You irresponsible man."
...A sigh escaped me despite myself.
I was the fool for expecting anything. Had this man still not grasped the gravity of the situation?

...Though it was not unreasonable. Keiichi did not know much about the faith surrounding Oyashiro-sama to begin with. Much less the reason for Rena's particular, obsessive dread of Oyashiro-sama—or the circumstances behind it.
The fact that he had trusted my words this far was already something close to a miracle.
Even so... the matter of Oyashiro-sama troubled me.

It was true that among all of us, Rena had the strongest consciousness of Oyashiro-sama. It was something I kept absolutely secret from Keiichi, but she had even undergone counseling from a doctor because of it.
And beyond that... I had also heard that she had caused trouble at her school after transferring, and had been forced to transfer yet again.

Hinamizawa Syndrome carries a significantly increased risk of symptom aggravation when the afflicted person leaves the village. The trigger is intense stress—the accumulation of suspicion, anxiety, and hatred in a short period, loading the mind beyond its limits.

Rena, a native of this village, found herself confronted with her parents' divorce after moving away, and her stress built to its breaking point. After onset, she fell into a state approaching L5—even if temporarily—and found herself in a genuinely dangerous condition.
...The progression was far beyond what the medications she had been taking could suppress. At one point, she had been given a prognosis of poor recovery.

And yet, fortunately—once Rena returned to Hinamizawa, she was selected as a test subject for the new drug that Irie had only just developed at that time, and underwent intensive treatment at the warehouse.
As a result, Rena managed to recover to the point where she could lead a mostly ordinary daily life—and that was how things stood now.
During that period, I had a chance to secretly glimpse a portion of the report that Irie had compiled for 'Tokyo'—and written there was a reference to 'Oyashiro-sama'—

Rena, it seemed, had interpreted every abnormality she experienced as the result of 'Oyashiro-sama's curse' that she had heard about as a small child, and had sought the cause in her own failings as someone who loved her family.
...This was not something that could occur in the ordinary way of a child her age shifting blame to something else—it revealed just how gentle Rena was at heart, how deeply she loved her family...
...And how her strong sense of independence had caused her thinking to turn inward and punishing. That was how the report had framed it.
In other words—the 'Oyashiro-sama' Rena spoke of was, in essence, Rena's own subconscious.
...If that was so, then a contradiction arose.
If the Oyashiro-sama Rena now spoke of truly reflected her latent feelings—would it be telling her to set aside her hostility?
—No. Quite the opposite. It should be inciting her further—urging the elimination of Rina, the perceived threat to her family—feeding her murderous intent.
And yet this time... Oyashiro-sama seemed to be redirecting Rena's impulse to protect her family away from exclusion, toward acceptance.
...If that was the case, this was not coming from Rena—or rather, not from Rena's own subconscious. Because Rena's raw, unguarded subconscious could not have wished to accept Rina.
Then... who, exactly, was this 'Oyashiro-sama' that had come to Rena again—this entity she had just spoken of?
"...chan, Rika-chan...?"
Someone called to me at close range, and I jolted in surprise. Looking up, I found Keiichi peering at my face with a worried look.
"What's wrong? You suddenly went somewhere far away..."
"Mii... it's nothing at all. More importantly—what do we do now?"
"For the time being... let's call it a day here. If we push any further, we'll only put Rena in a worse mood again."
...Frustrating, but he was right. Swallowing my unease and bitterness, I breathed out and nodded.
"...It can't be helped. Let's come back to this—and think carefully about what to do."
"...Even so, Rena's taking a long time. I wonder what happened?"
changeReason: OLD adds 'with this delivery guy' not in JPN; the original just says Rena is late (レナのやつ遅いな).

TextENGNew: From downstairs came heavy thuds—as though something large were being carried in.
"Let me just go and—?"
Keiichi had barely started to rise to his feet when the door opened and Rena looked in.
"You took a while... ...Rena?"

Seeing Rena's face, Rika-chan and I exchanged a glance. Perhaps her earlier exhaustion was to blame—but Rena's expression was hollow and dazed, as though she couldn't believe what she had seen, almost on the verge of tears.
"H-hey... what happened?"
"Rena, what is it? ...Did something happen?"
"Th-there's, um. Furniture... there's..."
"Furniture? What about furniture?"
"It seems like Dad ordered a lot of furniture... Rena had no idea..."
Her lips trembling, Rena stared with unseeing eyes. Perhaps moved by the sight, Keiichi put an arm around her shoulders to comfort her and said—
"Maybe your dad was planning to rearrange the house in secret? Like a surprise for you?"
"But... none of it is anything Dad would choose..."
There was something in Rena's voice—the tone of someone who had already formed a hypothesis inside them, but had not yet said it aloud.
"...Dad would never pick furniture that flashy..."
"—Rika-chan."
With a shared glance, Keiichi and I followed Rena downstairs.
From the truck, furniture was being carried in piece after piece in what seemed like a endless stream. There must have been no instructions for placement—items were set down wherever there was space.
The warm, unified tones of Rena's home were overtaken in an instant by all this brand-new furniture.

Pastel-colored chairs, rugs in a tropical style that were evidently about to be laid down. Everything looked stylish in the way one might see on a television drama—but inside this house, it was deeply out of place.
The feeling of it was jarring—as though a new resident had just moved in and displaced everyone who had lived there before.
"That should be everything—thank you for your patience!"

A voice from the front door announced the end of the work, followed by the sound of an engine starting up and the truck's gradual withdrawal. It had been like a sudden storm breaking over Rena's home.
Rena sat on the floor, somewhat stupefied, and did not move.
"Why would there be this much expensive furniture...?"
"Rena..."
Rena shot to her feet as though struck by a sudden thought and rushed into the inner room. Keiichi and I followed after her as though pulled.
"Here it is. This..."
Rena was opening the drawer of her father's desk with shaking hands.
She pulled out a heavy metal cashbox from beneath a stack of documents, aligned the combination without hesitation—as though she had memorized it—and opened it. A sharp breath escaped her.
"What is this..."
From beneath several bankbooks, a seal stamp, leftover postage stamps, and unused postcards, bundles of crisp ten-thousand-yen notes appeared.
Bundles of considerable thickness. Beside them, a number of paper bank bands—the kind used to bundle notes—were crammed in carelessly.
...The fact that only the bands remained meant the contents had already been spent long ago. The bands bore a stamp, hinting that each had once held a million yen.
As cash to find in a household lockbox, this was plainly an extraordinary sum. Everyone in the room could feel it.
And even that money—substantial as it was—seemed already abnormal. Perhaps unwilling to accept what it implied, Rena's trembling hand reached into the cashbox and opened one of the bankbooks.
Rena stared at the bankbook.
I stretched to look over her shoulder—then stopped myself. Even so, Rena's expression alone told me everything I needed to know.
For several tens of seconds, Rena kept her eyes fixed on the pages, motionless.
And then.
"...What is this..."
She whispered in a kind of stupefied disbelief, let both hands fall limp at her sides, and sank down where she stood.
"Rena..."
I crouched down in front of Rena and took hold of her loosely open hand.
As though in answer, Rena silently extended to me the bankbook she held in her other hand.
I looked inside. ...Sums in the hundreds of thousands had been withdrawn, again and again, over a short period of time.
And not only that. What appeared to have been a fixed-term savings deposit—set aside, perhaps, for the future—had been dissolved quite recently and folded into the ordinary account.
"...This can't be real."
Rena murmured with a crestfallen air. But the denial in her voice had none of the force she had carried until moments ago.
"—Hey, Rena."
Suddenly, from above, Keiichi seemed to have found something—he unfolded what appeared to be a document and asked Rena—
"You're... moving?"
"What?"
At Rena's startled reaction, Keiichi held out the document he had been holding.
The document was—
—an apartment brochure from a real estate company in Okinomiya.
From somewhere in the distance, the cry of higurashi cicadas.
Against the red evening sky, black crows calling back and forth.
Three long shadows stretched out ahead of us as we walked in silence.
The dry rattling sound of a bicycle wheel.
The sound of footsteps—halting, forlorn, uncertain.
We had visited the real estate agent in Okinomiya whose address was written on the brochure.
What greeted us there was a situation that had progressed even further than what Shion had told us—
—and a new, immovable fact.
"Oh, Mamiya Ritsuko-san's property? Yes, the reservation is confirmed."
The discovery that Rina Mamiya's real name was something else came as a small shock.
...That she would not give her real name to the man she claimed to be marrying—by any ordinary logic, that was impossible.

But as the exchange with the agent continued, Rena's color—already pale—drained past white into something ashen and gray, while Keiichi's face behind her flushed a deep red with barely contained fury.
...I must have worn the same expression as Keiichi. When I spread my palm open, I could still see the fresh marks where my nails had dug in from how tightly I had been clenching my fist.
Rina Mamiya—Mamiya Ritsuko, her real name—had planned to live in that apartment with another man entirely.
The evidence was there in the application: the name listed as co-resident was someone neither Rena's father nor Teppei Houjou—an entirely different person, a stranger.

...Apparently she had intended to cut ties with Teppei as well, once she had finished wringing money from Rena's father. When you thought about it, the fact that she hadn't brought Teppei in on the scheme to steal from the Sonozaki tribute was not surprising at all.
``But that apartment—isn't the price more than what she got from the savings deposit?``
changeReason: JPN 定期預金 means 'fixed-term deposit/savings'; OLD's 'investment' is less precise; also OLD uses dialogue format while JPN uses inner monologue.

TextENGNew: Keiichi's murmur let everything click into place. In other words—Rina had planned to make up the shortfall by stealing the Sonozaki tribute money.
Breathtaking greed. Shamelessness beyond belief. ...If not for the matter of Satoko, she was the kind of woman even I could have wanted to kill.
"...This can't be true..."
"—Rena?"
"It's not true, it's not true, it's not trueeee!! Aah, aaaahhhh!! Uwaaaaaaahh~~~!!!"
...After that, we somehow managed to calm the sobbing Rena down and left the real estate office.
Along the way, misfortune fell on one of the three: only Rena's bicycle had a broken chain, and we walked home with her, pushing the bicycle alongside.
"Rena..."

I walked alongside Rena, pushing the bicycle, and peered at her face. The sobbing had finally subsided, but the skin around her eyes was swollen and red, and her soft, rosy cheeks were faintly smudged with mud from when she had stumbled.
"I want you to... be all right..."
"...Why, I wonder...?"
She said it, and—
—Rena's feet stopped. Keiichi and I paused a few steps ahead and turned back to face her.
"...Rena followed what Oyashiro-sama said... I hated Rina-san, and I was completely against Dad remarrying, but..."
"Oyashiro-sama said I mustn't dislike her, I mustn't run away... so I decided to endure it. I believed that if I did that, I'd be able to be happy... I believed it..."
"Rena..."

"And I said a lot of terrible things to you too, Keiichi-kun...? Because I wasn't supposed to trust you... if I trusted what Keiichi-kun said, I'd start disliking Rina-san again, and want to oppose the marriage..."
"So I said things that hurt you... a lot of things. I'm sorry, I'm sorry—you'll never forgive me, I know, but please don't say any more... And then, even so... even so—!!"
Tears welled from Rena's eyes in great drops, rolling down her cheeks and falling to the earth at her feet. Watching her, I found myself remembering something Keiichi had said earlier.
"Rena does have a tendency—once she's convinced herself of something, she can be surprisingly stubborn. ...Or rather, she tends to lock herself into whatever she's decided to believe."
...Ah. So that was it.
Afraid of Oyashiro-sama, unable to believe that anyone would take her seriously—that was what lay behind that resistance of hers that had almost felt like lashing out.

And now I understood. Even while she was angry, even while she was struggling with the contradictions inside herself, confused and lost—Rena had been carrying guilt toward Keiichi and me all along, and the wish to apologize.
That was why.
"...Yeah, I knew."
And so it felt natural when Keiichi stepped closer to Rena, stroked her head, and held her.
"...Hey, Rena. I think Oyashiro-sama wasn't wrong. It's just that you misunderstood."
"What...?"
"You just said it yourself—Oyashiro-sama told you not to dislike her, not to run away. ...I don't think that means accepting your dad's remarriage."
"W-why is that...? Why?"

"Don't dislike her means... don't hate her. I mean, honestly—I hate Rina enough that I could kill her too. But I think what Oyashiro-sama meant was: don't let that feeling grow until you want to wipe someone out of existence entirely."

changeReason: JPN uses 「「 brackets indicating both Rika and Rena speaking simultaneously; OLD preserves this with double-pair quotes but NEW loses the dual-speaker indication.

TextENGNew: Rena and I both made the same sound at the same moment. And then I looked steadily at Keiichi's face.
"As for 'don't run away'—I think that one's pretty straightforward: don't leave a problem sitting there unaddressed."
"If you truly care about your family, then fight. ...Not fight Rina, mind you. Having a serious, honest conversation with your dad—that's one kind of fighting too."
"If that's hard—talk it over with someone you can trust. I think that's what Oyashiro-sama was trying to say."
"Keiichi-kun..."
"...Keiichi."
"...I thought killing those two was the right thing to do. ...But from the day I killed them... I think the world had already started to go wrong."
"...Which means... Was killing those two... not the right thing after all..."
"I can't say whether it was right or wrong—but it certainly wasn't the best move."
"...The best move?"
``Can't you figure that out on your own?!``
changeReason: JPN そんなこともわからないのか is an exasperated exclamation ('you can't even figure that out?!'); OLD's mild 'Don't you know?' loses the emotional intensity.

TextENGNew: I pulled Rena into my arms and held her, pressing her against my chest.
And then I told her.
It was something very simple.
A very simple option that anyone could have thought of.
But Rena hadn't.
I hadn't either, back then.
"When something scares you, when you start to suspect, when something hurts—"
It really is such a simple thing...!
"...Talk to your friends! Tell them what's wrong!!"
"...You're right..."
"...You're right... uh... uwaaaaaaah..."
For a while, Rena wept in my arms—mourning the choice she had gotten wrong at the very beginning.
It was not an option that had been difficult to find.
...It was right there, close at hand, as simple as it could be.
How utterly pointless it was to carry the burden alone.
She had friends.
Friends she could trust with everything.
And yet—why had she convinced herself this was something she couldn't share with them...?
"...I wanted you to remember. The trust you found in those irreplaceable friends."
"No one sees it as a burden when you want to lean on them. I wanted you to understand that. ...Otherwise, you would surely come to regret it..."
...Until now, I had thought all my misfortune was the result of 'Oyashiro-sama's curse.' So I swore that from here on I would believe in what Oyashiro-sama said, and follow it.
But... that too was wrong. I had been placing the blame for everything on 'Oyashiro-sama,' and pushing the painful things off into later.
...And then, like a snowball rolling downhill, it grew and grew until there was nothing to be done—and finally I wept, and raged, and in the end destroyed myself—!
"...That's right... yeah... you're right—!"

"Have a real conversation with your dad, Rena. The Rena who quietly worries about other people's feelings is wonderful—but the Rena who smiles and laughs from the bottom of her heart is the one I love most."
"...Yeah... yeah—! Thank you... thank you, Keiichi-kun, Rika-chan—!!"
Nodding, Rena sobbed again through her tears. But these tears—they were warm, unlike the ones from before.
"...Ah, there you are. Hey, Kei-chaaaan!"
At the sound of a voice, we looked up and turned. From behind a shed in the distance, Mion was waving and walking toward us.
"Oooh! Thanks for coming to get us, former chairperson."
"How gracious of you, current chairperson. ...Heh heh heh!"
She gave an exaggerated salute with a grin. Then she turned to Rena, softened, and gave her a pat on the shoulder.
"...Cheer up, Rena. That kind of face doesn't suit you."
"...Mii-chan..."
"Now then—Grandma's waiting. And there's mountains of food waiting too~?"
"...What? K-Keiichi-kun, this is...?"
"Oh, I made a call from a payphone on the way—reserved one suite at the finest inn in the area."
``I got in touch with Rena's dad while we were at it! It's been forever since we all got rowdy together~!``
changeReason: OLD loses JPN ひっさびさに (for the first time in a long while) and changes the tone from nostalgic reunion to generic fun suggestion.

TextENGNew: The two of them looked at each other and nodded without a word. A feast, a sleepover at Mion's place... They had had all of this arranged from the very beginning?
The sheer efficiency of it left me briefly speechless. ...Keiichi. Just how many times do you intend to move me like this?
At last, a smile broke across Rena's face. At the sight of her joy, I felt the whole day's exhaustion begin, somehow, to melt away.
